1:
"Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, according to the promise of life which is in Christ Jesus," The witnesses note the greeting: an apostle by God's will, according to the promise of life. On "Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God," the grammatical voices note the authority. Barnes and Robertson note Paul opens his last letter with his apostolic title and its ground: "an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God" — his office rests not on human appointment but on the will of God, who called him; the same formula opens several of his letters.

On "according to the promise of life which is in Christ Jesus," the witnesses note the keynote. Barnes and Calvin note the distinctive phrase: his apostleship is "according to the promise of life (the promise of eternal life) which is in Christ Jesus" — his calling is in the service of the promise of life; the gospel he is sent to proclaim is the good news of life in Christ, fitting for a letter written facing death.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the comfort: Paul, awaiting martyrdom, opens with the promise of life — the life that death cannot touch, held out in Christ. The verse displays the greeting: an apostle by God's will, according to the promise of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul opens with his title and its ground — "an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God," his office resting on God's will not human appointment (Barnes, Robertson); "according to the promise of life which is in Christ Jesus," his apostleship in the service of the promise of eternal life, the keynote of a letter facing death (Barnes, Calvin); the dying apostle opening with the life death cannot touch (Chrysostom). The verse displays the greeting. The verse marks the office: an apostle. The verse displays the ground: God's will. The verse displays the keynote: the promise of life. The verse displays the source: in Christ Jesus. The greeting displays Paul's apostleship in the service of life. The verse displays the apostle: Paul. The verse displays the will: God's. The verse displays the promise: of life. The greeting — an apostle by God's will, according to the promise of life — displays the opening of Paul's final letter: he writes as an apostle of Christ by God's will, in the service of the promise of eternal life which is in Christ Jesus — the life that crowns even his approaching death. The verse displays the apostolic greeting, addressed to his beloved son Timothy (v.2).
2:
"To Timothy, my dearly beloved son: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord." The brief verse notes the address to Timothy with grace, mercy, and peace. The grammatical voices note the affection. Barnes and Robertson note the tender address: "to Timothy, my dearly beloved (agapeto — beloved) son" — Paul writes to his spiritual child, his closest companion, with deep affection; Timothy was a son in the faith, begotten through the gospel.

On "grace, mercy, and peace," the witnesses note the threefold blessing. Barnes and Calvin note the greeting, fuller than the usual "grace and peace": "grace, mercy, and peace" — to grace (free favour) and peace (its fruit) is added "mercy," fitting for a young minister needing God's compassion and help in his trials.

On "from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord," the grammatical-historical note the joint source: the blessing comes "from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord" — Father and Son the joint source, a witness to Christ's divine dignity. The verse displays the address to Timothy with grace, mercy, and peace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the tender address — "to Timothy, my dearly beloved (beloved) son," his spiritual child and closest companion (Barnes, Robertson); the threefold blessing "grace, mercy, and peace," "mercy" added for a young minister needing compassion in his trials (Barnes, Calvin); "from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord," Father and Son the joint source (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the greeting. The verse marks the address: beloved son. The verse displays the blessing: grace, mercy, peace. The verse displays the addition: mercy. The verse displays the source: Father and Son. The greeting displays Paul blessing his beloved son. The verse displays the Timothy: beloved. The verse displays the mercy: added. The verse displays the source: God and Christ. The address to Timothy with grace, mercy, and peace displays the warmth of Paul's final letter: he writes to Timothy, his dearly beloved son in the faith, invoking grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord. The verse displays the greeting, opening into thanksgiving for Timothy (vv.3-5).
3:
"I thank God, whom I serve from my forefathers with pure conscience, that without ceasing I have remembrance of thee in my prayers night and day;" The witnesses note Paul's constant thankful remembrance of Timothy in prayer. On "I thank God, whom I serve from my forefathers with pure conscience," the grammatical voices note the worship. Barnes and Robertson note Paul opens with thanks: "I thank God, whom I serve from my forefathers (in the line of his pious ancestors, as a worshipper of the God of Israel) with pure conscience" — he serves the same God his fathers served, with a clear conscience, the gospel being the fulfilment not the abandonment of his fathers' faith.

On "without ceasing I have remembrance of thee in my prayers," the witnesses note the intercession. Barnes and Calvin note the constancy: "without ceasing I have remembrance (remembrance, mention) of thee in my prayers" — Timothy was continually in Paul's prayers; he remembered him without intermission.

On "night and day," the puritan Henry notes the diligence: Paul prayed "night and day" — by night and by day, constantly; the aged apostle, even in prison, kept up unceasing intercession for his beloved son. The verse displays Paul's constant thankful remembrance of Timothy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul opens with thanks — "I thank God, whom I serve from my forefathers with pure conscience," serving the God of his fathers with a clear conscience (Barnes, Robertson); "without ceasing I have remembrance of thee in my prayers," Timothy continually in his prayers (Barnes, Calvin); "night and day," constant intercession even in prison (Henry). The verse displays his remembrance. The verse marks the thanks: I thank God. The verse displays the service: from his forefathers. The verse displays the remembrance: without ceasing. The verse displays the constancy: night and day. The remembrance displays Paul praying continually for Timothy. The verse displays the God: served. The verse displays the conscience: pure. The verse displays the prayers: night and day. Paul's constant thankful remembrance of Timothy in prayer displays the love that opens his letter: serving the God of his fathers with a pure conscience, he gives thanks and remembers Timothy without ceasing in his prayers, night and day. The verse displays the apostle's intercession, longing to see Timothy's face (v.4).
4:
"Greatly desiring to see thee, being mindful of thy tears, that I may be filled with joy;" The brief verse notes Paul's longing to see Timothy and be filled with joy. The grammatical voices note the yearning. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's deep desire for reunion: "greatly desiring to see thee" — the aged, imprisoned apostle longed to see his beloved son once more before his death; the yearning of a father for his child.

On "being mindful of thy tears," the witnesses note the parting memory. Barnes and Ellicott note the touching recollection: "being mindful of thy tears" — Paul remembered the tears Timothy had shed, probably at their last parting; the memory of that grief deepened his longing to see him again.

On "that I may be filled with joy," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the hoped-for gladness: the reunion would fill Paul "with joy" — the sight of Timothy would turn his sorrow to joy; love made the longed-for meeting a source of deep gladness. The verse displays Paul's longing to see Timothy and be filled with joy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's deep desire — "greatly desiring to see thee," the imprisoned apostle longing to see his son once more (Barnes, Robertson); "being mindful of thy tears," remembering Timothy's tears at their parting, the memory deepening his longing (Barnes, Ellicott); "that I may be filled with joy," the reunion turning his sorrow to gladness (Wesley). The verse displays his longing. The verse marks the desire: to see thee. The verse displays the memory: thy tears. The verse displays the hope: filled with joy. The verse displays the affection: father and son. The longing displays Paul yearning for joyful reunion. The verse displays the desire: great. The verse displays the tears: remembered. The verse displays the joy: hoped for. Paul's longing to see Timothy and be filled with joy displays the tenderness of his heart: the aged prisoner greatly desired to see his beloved son once more — remembering the tears of their parting — that the reunion might fill him with joy. The verse displays the apostle's yearning, grounded in Timothy's unfeigned faith (v.5).
5:
"When I call to remembrance the unfeigned faith that is in thee, which dwelt first in thy grandmother Lois, and thy mother Eunice; and I am persuaded that in thee also." The witnesses note Timothy's unfeigned faith, inherited through Lois and Eunice. On "the unfeigned faith that is in thee," the grammatical voices note the sincerity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's recollection: "the unfeigned (anhypokritos — sincere, without hypocrisy, genuine) faith that is in thee" — Timothy's faith was real and unfeigned, not a pretence; a faith Paul remembered with thankfulness.

On "which dwelt first in thy grandmother Lois, and thy mother Eunice," the witnesses note the godly heritage. Barnes and Calvin note the line of faith: it "dwelt first in thy grandmother Lois, and thy mother Eunice" — Timothy's faith had a godly ancestry, passed down through his grandmother and mother (Jewish believers, Acts 16:1); the influence of pious women in his upbringing.

On "I am persuaded that in thee also," the puritan Henry notes the confidence: "I am persuaded that in thee also" — Paul is confident the same genuine faith now dwells in Timothy; the heritage of faith had borne fruit in the son. The verse displays Timothy's unfeigned faith, inherited through Lois and Eunice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's recollection — "the unfeigned (sincere, genuine) faith that is in thee," Timothy's faith real not a pretence (Barnes, Robertson); "which dwelt first in thy grandmother Lois, and thy mother Eunice," a godly heritage passed through pious women (Acts 16:1, Barnes, Calvin); "I am persuaded that in thee also," confident the same faith now dwelt in Timothy (Henry). The verse displays the inherited faith. The verse marks the faith: unfeigned. The verse displays the line: Lois and Eunice. The verse displays the heritage: godly women. The verse displays the confidence: in thee also. The faith displays Timothy's sincere faith passed down through his family. The verse displays the faith: genuine. The verse displays the grandmother: Lois. The verse displays the mother: Eunice. Timothy's unfeigned faith, inherited through Lois and Eunice, displays the godly heritage Paul recalls: a sincere, genuine faith that had dwelt first in his grandmother Lois and his mother Eunice, and now, Paul is persuaded, dwelt in Timothy himself. The verse displays the inherited faith, which Timothy is to stir up (v.6).
6:
"Wherefore I put thee in remembrance that thou stir up the gift of God, which is in thee by the putting on of my hands." The witnesses note the charge to stir up the gift received by the laying on of hands. On "I put thee in remembrance that thou stir up the gift of God," the grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge: "stir up (anazopyrein — rekindle, fan into flame, as one stirs a dying fire to a blaze) the gift of God which is in thee" — Timothy is to rouse and rekindle the spiritual gift God gave him, that it not grow cold or dim through neglect.

On "the gift of God which is in thee," the witnesses note the endowment. Barnes and Calvin note the "gift (charisma)" — the spiritual endowment for ministry given to Timothy, whether a special grace or the gifts needed for his office; a gift to be exercised and kept aflame, not left idle.

On "by the putting on of my hands," the puritan Henry notes the occasion: the gift came "by the putting on of my hands" — at Timothy's ordination, when Paul (with the presbytery, 1 Timothy 4:14) laid hands on him; the laying on of hands the solemn occasion of his commissioning and gift. The verse displays the charge to stir up the gift.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge — "stir up (rekindle, fan into flame) the gift of God which is in thee," rousing the gift that it not grow cold through neglect (Barnes, Robertson); the "gift (charisma)," the spiritual endowment for ministry, to be exercised not left idle (Barnes, Calvin); "by the putting on of my hands," given at his ordination when Paul laid hands on him (Henry). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the exhortation: stir up the gift. The verse displays the figure: fan into flame. The verse displays the gift: of God. The verse displays the occasion: laying on of hands. The charge displays Timothy to rekindle his ministerial gift. The verse displays the stirring: up. The verse displays the gift: God's. The verse displays the hands: laid on. The charge to stir up the gift received by the laying on of hands displays Paul's call to faithfulness: Timothy is to rekindle and fan into flame the gift of God that was given him at his ordination, by the laying on of Paul's hands — keeping it aflame, not letting it grow cold. The verse displays the call to rekindle the gift, grounded in the Spirit God gives (v.7).
7:
"For God hath not given us the spirit of fear; but of power, and of love, and of a sound mind." The witnesses note the spirit God gives: power, love, and a sound mind, not fear. On "God hath not given us the spirit of fear," the grammatical voices note the negative. Barnes and Robertson note the ground for stirring up the gift (and for boldness): "God hath not given us the spirit of fear (deilias — cowardice, timidity)" — the Spirit God gives is not one of cowardly fear; timidity in ministry is not from God.

On "but of power, and of love," the witnesses note the first two graces. Barnes and Calvin note the positive: "but of power (dynamis — strength, courage, efficacy) and of love (agape — love to God and men)" — the Spirit gives strength to act and endure, and love that animates and softens; power and love together.

On "and of a sound mind," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the third: "and of a sound mind (sophronismos — self-discipline, sober-mindedness, sound judgment)" — a disciplined, self-controlled, sober spirit; the threefold gift — power, love, and sound discipline — equips for faithful ministry. The verse displays the spirit God gives: power, love, and a sound mind.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the negative — "God hath not given us the spirit of fear (cowardice, timidity)," timidity not from God (Barnes, Robertson); the positive "power (strength, courage) and love (to God and men)," strength to endure and love to animate (Barnes, Calvin); "a sound mind (self-discipline, sober judgment)," a disciplined, self-controlled spirit (Wesley). The verse displays the Spirit's gift. The verse marks the negative: not fear. The verse displays the first: power. The verse displays the second: love. The verse displays the third: a sound mind. The gift displays the Spirit equipping for bold, loving, disciplined ministry. The verse displays the fear: not given. The verse displays the power: given. The verse displays the sound mind: given. The spirit God gives — power, love, and a sound mind, not fear — displays the ground of Timothy's boldness: the Spirit God bestows is not one of cowardly fear but of power to endure, love to animate, and sound self-discipline; with such a Spirit, Timothy need not be timid. The verse displays the Spirit's threefold gift, summoning him not to be ashamed (v.8).
8:
"Be not thou therefore ashamed of the testimony of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner: but be thou partaker of the afflictions of the gospel according to the power of God;" The witnesses note the charge not to be ashamed but to share the gospel's afflictions. On "be not thou therefore ashamed of the testimony of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner," the grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge (drawn from the Spirit of power, v.7): "be not thou ashamed of the testimony (the witness, the gospel) of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner" — Timothy is not to be ashamed of the gospel, nor of Paul, now a prisoner for it; the chains were no disgrace.

On "of me his prisoner," the eastern Chrysostom notes the title. Chrysostom and Calvin note that Paul calls himself "his prisoner (the prisoner of the Lord)" — not Nero's prisoner but Christ's; his bonds were for Christ, an honour not a shame, and Timothy must not be ashamed of them.

On "be thou partaker of the afflictions of the gospel according to the power of God," the grammatical-historical note the call to suffer: rather than being ashamed, Timothy is to "be thou partaker of the afflictions (synkakopatheson — suffer hardship together with) of the gospel according to the power of God" — to share in the sufferings the gospel brings, sustained by God's power. The verse displays the charge not to be ashamed but to share the gospel's afflictions.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge — "be not ashamed of the testimony (gospel) of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner," not ashamed of the gospel or of Paul's chains (Barnes, Robertson); "his prisoner," Christ's prisoner not Nero's, his bonds an honour (Chrysostom, Calvin); "be thou partaker of the afflictions of the gospel according to the power of God," sharing its sufferings sustained by God's power (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the negative: be not ashamed. The verse displays the objects: the gospel and Paul. The verse displays the prisoner: of the Lord. The verse displays the call: share its afflictions. The charge displays Timothy unashamed and sharing the gospel's sufferings. The verse displays the shame: refused. The verse displays the prisoner: Christ's. The verse displays the afflictions: shared. The charge not to be ashamed but to share the gospel's afflictions displays the boldness Paul urges: Timothy is not to be ashamed of the gospel or of Paul, the Lord's prisoner, but to take his share in the sufferings the gospel brings — sustained by the power of God. The verse displays the call to suffer, grounded in God's saving grace (v.9).
9:
"Who hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling, not according to our works, but according to his own purpose and grace, which was given us in Christ Jesus before the world began," The witnesses note salvation by God's own purpose and grace, not our works. On "who hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling," the grammatical voices note the work of grace. Barnes and Robertson note the description of the God whose power sustains: he "hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling (a calling to holiness, a holy summons)" — God both saves and calls, the calling being holy in its source, nature, and end.

On "not according to our works, but according to his own purpose and grace," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes and Calvin note the great antithesis: salvation is "not according to our works (not earned, not for any merit of ours) but according to his own purpose (his eternal design) and grace (free favour)" — the Reformed press this as the foundation of salvation by grace alone, grounded in God's eternal purpose, excluding all human merit.

On "which was given us in Christ Jesus before the world began," the Reformed note the eternity: this grace "was given us in Christ Jesus before the world began (before times eternal)" — the purpose of grace is eternal, formed in Christ before time; the believer's salvation rests on God's eternal choice in Christ. The verse displays salvation by God's own purpose and grace, not our works.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the work of grace — God "hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling," both saving and calling to holiness (Barnes, Robertson); the antithesis "not according to our works but according to his own purpose and grace," salvation by grace alone, grounded in God's eternal purpose, excluding merit (Barnes, Calvin); "given us in Christ Jesus before the world began," the purpose eternal, formed in Christ before time (Reformed). The verse displays salvation by grace. The verse marks the work: saved and called. The verse displays the calling: holy. The verse displays the ground: not works but grace. The verse displays the eternity: before the world began. The salvation displays grace, not merit, grounded in God's eternal purpose. The verse displays the saving: God's. The verse displays the grace: free. The verse displays the purpose: eternal. Salvation by God's own purpose and grace, not our works, displays the foundation of the gospel: God saved and called us with a holy calling — not for any works of ours, but according to his own eternal purpose and grace, given us in Christ Jesus before the world began. The verse displays the eternal grace, now made manifest in Christ's appearing (v.10).
10:
"But is now made manifest by the appearing of our Saviour Jesus Christ, who hath abolished death, and hath brought life and immortality to light through the gospel:" The witnesses note Christ abolishing death and bringing life and immortality to light. On "now made manifest by the appearing of our Saviour Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the disclosure. Barnes and Robertson note that the eternal purpose of grace (v.9), long hidden, is "now made manifest (revealed, brought to light) by the appearing (epiphaneia — the manifestation, the first advent) of our Saviour Jesus Christ" — what was purposed before time is now disclosed in Christ's coming.

On "who hath abolished death," the witnesses note the victory. Barnes and Calvin note the triumph: Christ "hath abolished (katargesantos — brought to nought, rendered powerless, annulled) death" — by his own death and resurrection he has broken death's power; death is not destroyed in fact yet but its dominion and sting are abolished for the believer.

On "hath brought life and immortality to light through the gospel," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the gift: he "hath brought life and immortality (incorruption) to light through the gospel" — the gospel has illumined and made clear the life and immortality dimly seen before; Christ has brought to light the certainty of eternal, incorruptible life. The verse displays Christ abolishing death and bringing life and immortality to light.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the disclosure — the eternal purpose "now made manifest (revealed) by the appearing (manifestation) of our Saviour Jesus Christ," disclosed in Christ's coming (Barnes, Robertson); "who hath abolished (annulled, rendered powerless) death," breaking death's dominion by his death and resurrection (Barnes, Calvin); "hath brought life and immortality (incorruption) to light through the gospel," illumining the certainty of eternal life (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the victory over death. The verse marks the disclosure: now made manifest. The verse displays the appearing: of the Saviour. The verse displays the victory: abolished death. The verse displays the gift: life and immortality. The disclosure displays Christ revealing grace and conquering death. The verse displays the manifesting: of grace. The verse displays the death: abolished. The verse displays the life: brought to light. Christ abolishing death and bringing life and immortality to light displays the manifestation of the eternal grace: the purpose hidden before time is now revealed by the appearing of our Saviour Jesus Christ, who has annulled the power of death and brought the certainty of life and immortality to light through the gospel. The verse displays the gospel's great gift, which Paul was appointed to preach (v.11).
11:
"Whereunto I am appointed a preacher, and an apostle, and a teacher of the Gentiles." The brief verse notes Paul's appointment as preacher, apostle, and teacher of the Gentiles. The grammatical voices note the threefold office. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's account of his commission for the gospel just described: "whereunto (for which gospel) I am appointed (placed, ordained) a preacher (herald, kerux), and an apostle (one sent), and a teacher of the Gentiles" — three terms for his one office in the service of the gospel.

On the three titles, the witnesses note their distinct force. Barnes and Ellicott note the threefold designation: "preacher" (a herald proclaiming the message), "apostle" (one commissioned and sent with authority), and "teacher" (one instructing in the truth) — the same that Paul gives in 1 Timothy 2:7; the fullness of his commission.

On "of the Gentiles," the grammatical-historical note the sphere: especially "of the Gentiles" — Paul's distinctive calling was to the nations; the gospel of life was through him brought to the Gentile world. The verse displays Paul's appointment as preacher, apostle, and teacher.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's commission — "whereunto (for which gospel) I am appointed a preacher (herald), and an apostle (one sent), and a teacher of the Gentiles," three terms for his one office (Barnes, Robertson); the distinct force — herald, commissioned apostle, and teacher (as 1 Timothy 2:7, Barnes, Ellicott); "of the Gentiles," his distinctive calling to the nations (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his appointment. The verse marks the office: preacher, apostle, teacher. The verse displays the commission: appointed. The verse displays the sphere: the Gentiles. The verse displays the service: of the gospel. The appointment displays Paul's threefold office for the gospel. The verse displays the preacher: herald. The verse displays the apostle: sent. The verse displays the teacher: of the Gentiles. Paul's appointment as preacher, apostle, and teacher of the Gentiles displays his commission in the gospel of life: for that gospel he was appointed a herald to proclaim it, an apostle sent with authority, and a teacher to instruct — especially among the Gentiles. The verse displays the apostle's office, the cause of his sufferings (v.12).
12:
"For the which cause I also suffer these things: nevertheless I am not ashamed: for I know whom I have believed, and am persuaded that he is able to keep that which I have committed unto him against that day." The witnesses note Paul's confidence that Christ will keep his deposit — and the question of the deposit. On "I also suffer these things: nevertheless I am not ashamed," the grammatical voices note the unashamed suffering. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's testimony: "for the which cause (his apostleship to the Gentiles) I also suffer these things (his imprisonment): nevertheless I am not ashamed" — his sufferings did not shame him, for he knew the One he served.

On "I know whom I have believed," the witnesses note the personal trust. Barnes and Ellicott note the famous confession: "I know whom I have believed (trusted, and still trust)" — not merely what but whom; Paul's confidence rests on his personal knowledge of Christ (or the Father), whose power and faithfulness he had proved.

On "able to keep that which I have committed unto him," interpreters divide over "my deposit." The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, with Wesley, take "that which I have committed (ten paratheken mou — my deposit)" as Paul's own soul, his eternal interests, entrusted to God's keeping (cf. Psalm 31:5) — the deposit Paul placed in God's safe-keeping. Clarke notes the diversity of views: his life (to be received again at the resurrection), his soul, or the gospel itself, which God will preserve and proclaim by others (the passive sense, what God committed to Paul, linking to v.14). The verse displays Paul's confidence that Christ will keep his deposit.

The witnesses agree the substance: Paul, suffering for his apostleship, is not ashamed, for he knows whom he has trusted and is persuaded that he is "able to keep that which I have committed unto him against that day" — the day of Christ's coming (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott). But "my deposit" divides them: the majority (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, Wesley) take it as Paul's own soul and eternal interests, entrusted to God's safe-keeping (Psalm 31:5); Clarke notes others take it as his life (for the resurrection) or as the gospel itself, which God will preserve through others (linking to v.14). The verse displays the deposit crux. The verse marks the suffering: not ashamed. The verse displays the trust: I know whom I have believed. The verse displays the confidence: able to keep. The verse displays the debate: the soul or the gospel. The confidence displays Paul resting his deposit in Christ's keeping. The verse displays the believing: personal. The verse displays the deposit: kept. The verse displays the day: that day. Paul's confidence that Christ will keep his deposit displays the serenity of the dying apostle and an exegetical crux: suffering yet unashamed, he knows whom he has trusted and is sure he is able to keep his deposit — read as Paul's own soul entrusted to God (Barnes, Ellicott, Wesley) or the gospel God entrusted to him (Clarke notes) — safe against that day. The verse displays the apostle's confidence, charging Timothy to hold sound words (v.13).
13:
"Hold fast the form of sound words, which thou hast heard of me, in faith and love which is in Christ Jesus." The witnesses note the charge to hold fast the form of sound words in faith and love. On "hold fast the form of sound words," the grammatical voices note the standard. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge: "hold fast (echo — retain, keep as a pattern) the form (hypotyposis — outline, pattern, model) of sound (healthful, wholesome) words which thou hast heard of me" — Timothy is to keep the apostolic teaching, the body of sound doctrine, as a fixed pattern, not departing from it.

On "the form of sound words," the eastern Chrysostom notes the deposit of doctrine. Chrysostom and Calvin note that the "form (or outline) of sound words" is the summary of healthful apostolic teaching Timothy received from Paul; he is to hold it as a model and standard, guarding it against the unsound babblings of error.

On "in faith and love which is in Christ Jesus," the grammatical-historical note the spirit: the holding is to be "in faith and love which is in Christ Jesus" — not a dead, formal orthodoxy, but sound doctrine held in living faith and love; truth and grace together. The verse displays the charge to hold fast the form of sound words.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge — "hold fast (retain) the form (pattern, outline) of sound (wholesome) words which thou hast heard of me," keeping the apostolic teaching as a fixed pattern (Barnes, Robertson); the "form of sound words" the summary of healthful doctrine, guarded against error (Chrysostom, Calvin); "in faith and love which is in Christ Jesus," held in living faith and love, not dead orthodoxy (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the holding: hold fast. The verse displays the standard: the form of sound words. The verse displays the source: heard of me. The verse displays the spirit: faith and love. The charge displays Timothy keeping sound doctrine in faith and love. The verse displays the form: sound words. The verse displays the holding: fast. The verse displays the love: in Christ. The charge to hold fast the form of sound words in faith and love displays Paul's care for the deposit of truth: Timothy is to keep the pattern of sound, wholesome doctrine he received from Paul — holding it not as dead orthodoxy but in the living faith and love that are in Christ Jesus. The verse displays the charge to keep sound doctrine, guarded by the Spirit (v.14).
14:
"That good thing which was committed unto thee keep by the Holy Ghost which dwelleth in us." The witnesses note the charge to keep the good deposit by the Holy Ghost. On "that good thing which was committed unto thee," the grammatical voices note the deposit. Barnes and Robertson note the parallel to v.12: "that good thing which was committed unto thee (ten kalen paratheken — the good deposit)" — here clearly the deposit God committed to Timothy: the gospel, the sound doctrine, the trust of the ministry; the precious treasure entrusted to his keeping.

On "keep," the witnesses note the duty. Barnes and Calvin note the charge: "keep (phylaxon — guard, watch over, as a treasure against robbery)" — Timothy is to guard the deposit of truth, keeping it pure and unimpaired, defending it against the corruptions of error.

On "by the Holy Ghost which dwelleth in us," the eastern Cyril notes the power: the keeping is "by the Holy Ghost which dwelleth in us" — not by human strength alone but by the indwelling Spirit; the Spirit who dwells in believers enables the guarding of the truth. The verse displays the charge to keep the good deposit by the Holy Ghost.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deposit — "that good thing which was committed unto thee (the good deposit)," the gospel and sound doctrine God entrusted to Timothy (Barnes, Robertson); "keep (guard, watch over as a treasure)," guarding it pure against error's corruptions (Barnes, Calvin); "by the Holy Ghost which dwelleth in us," the keeping by the indwelling Spirit's power, not human strength alone (Cyril). The verse displays the guarded deposit. The verse marks the deposit: the good thing committed. The verse displays the duty: keep it. The verse displays the figure: guard a treasure. The verse displays the power: the Holy Ghost. The charge displays Timothy guarding the truth by the Spirit. The verse displays the deposit: good. The verse displays the keeping: guarded. The verse displays the Spirit: indwelling. The charge to keep the good deposit by the Holy Ghost displays the trust laid on Timothy: he is to guard the precious deposit of the gospel committed to him — keeping it pure against all corruption — by the power of the Holy Ghost who dwells in believers. The verse displays the guarded trust, against the background of widespread defection (v.15).
15:
"This thou knowest, that all they which are in Asia be turned away from me; of whom are Phygellus and Hermogenes." The brief verse notes the defection of those in Asia from Paul. The grammatical voices note the desertion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's sorrowful report: "all they which are in Asia (the Asian Christians at Rome, or the Asian believers generally) be turned away from me" — many who should have stood with him had deserted him; "all" probably means the general body, not every individual, especially in his hour of trial.

On the meaning of the turning away, the witnesses note the shame. Barnes and Henry note that they "turned away" likely out of fear — ashamed of Paul's chains (the very thing he charged Timothy not to be, v.8), they withdrew their support and fellowship when it became dangerous to be associated with him.

On "Phygellus and Hermogenes," the grammatical-historical note the named deserters: "of whom are Phygellus and Hermogenes" — two named (otherwise unknown), singled out perhaps as prominent or especially disappointing examples of the defection. The verse displays the defection of those in Asia from Paul.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's sorrowful report — "all they which are in Asia be turned away from me," many deserting him, "all" meaning the general body not every individual (Barnes, Robertson); they turned away out of fear, ashamed of his chains, withdrawing when it grew dangerous (Barnes, Henry); "Phygellus and Hermogenes," two named deserters, prominent examples (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the defection. The verse marks the desertion: turned away. The verse displays the place: Asia. The verse displays the cause: ashamed of his chains. The verse displays the named: Phygellus and Hermogenes. The defection displays many forsaking Paul in his trial. The verse displays the turning: away. The verse displays the Asia: deserting. The verse displays the names: two. The defection of those in Asia from Paul displays the loneliness of the aged apostle: in his hour of trial, the general body of the Asian believers had turned away from him — ashamed, it seems, of his chains — among them the two named Phygellus and Hermogenes. The verse displays the desertion, set against the faithfulness of Onesiphorus (vv.16-18).
16:
"The Lord give mercy unto the house of Onesiphorus; for he oft refreshed me, and was not ashamed of my chain:" The witnesses note the faithfulness of Onesiphorus, who was not ashamed of Paul's chain. On "the Lord give mercy unto the house of Onesiphorus," the grammatical voices note the prayer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's grateful prayer for his faithful friend's household: "the Lord give mercy unto the house (the family) of Onesiphorus" — Paul prays a blessing on the whole family of the one who had stood by him; the kindness shown to him he repays with prayer.

On "he oft refreshed me," the witnesses note the kindness. Barnes and Calvin note the service: Onesiphorus "oft (often, many times) refreshed (anepsyxe — revived, cheered, gave relief to) me" — he repeatedly brought comfort and relief to the imprisoned apostle; a faithful friend in his distress.

On "was not ashamed of my chain," the puritan Henry notes the courage: he "was not ashamed of my chain" — unlike those in Asia who turned away (v.15), Onesiphorus was not ashamed to be associated with the prisoner; he braved the danger and disgrace to stand by Paul. The verse displays the faithfulness of Onesiphorus.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's grateful prayer — "the Lord give mercy unto the house (family) of Onesiphorus," blessing the household of his faithful friend (Barnes, Robertson); "he oft refreshed (revived, cheered) me," repeatedly bringing relief to the prisoner (Barnes, Calvin); "was not ashamed of my chain," not ashamed to stand by the prisoner, unlike the deserters (Henry). The verse displays the faithful friend. The verse marks the prayer: mercy to his house. The verse displays the kindness: oft refreshed me. The verse displays the courage: not ashamed of my chain. The verse displays the contrast: with the deserters. The faithfulness displays Onesiphorus standing by Paul in his chains. The verse displays the mercy: prayed. The verse displays the refreshing: often. The verse displays the chain: unashamed. The faithfulness of Onesiphorus, who was not ashamed of Paul's chain, displays the bright contrast with the deserters: he often refreshed the imprisoned apostle and was not ashamed to be associated with his chains — for which Paul prays the Lord's mercy on his whole household. The verse displays the faithful friend, who sought Paul out at Rome (v.17).
17:
"But, when he was in Rome, he sought me out very diligently, and found me." The brief verse notes Onesiphorus seeking Paul out diligently at Rome. The grammatical voices note the diligent search. Barnes and Robertson note the measure of his devotion: "when he was in Rome, he sought me out very diligently (spoudaioteron — earnestly, with great effort), and found me" — Onesiphorus did not let Paul's imprisonment hide him; he searched hard until he located the apostle.

On "sought me out very diligently," the witnesses note the effort. Barnes and Ellicott note that to find a single prisoner in the vast city of Rome required real effort and persistence; the danger of seeking out a man accused before Nero made the search the more courageous; Onesiphorus spared no pains.

On "and found me," the grammatical-historical note the success: "and found me" — his diligent search succeeded; he reached Paul and ministered to him. His love overcame both difficulty and danger. The verse displays Onesiphorus seeking Paul out diligently at Rome.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the measure of his devotion — "when he was in Rome, he sought me out very diligently (earnestly, with great effort), and found me," not letting the imprisonment hide Paul (Barnes, Robertson); finding a single prisoner in vast Rome requiring real effort, the danger making it courageous (Barnes, Ellicott); "and found me," his search succeeding, his love overcoming difficulty and danger (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the diligent search. The verse marks the place: in Rome. The verse displays the effort: sought very diligently. The verse displays the difficulty: a prisoner in vast Rome. The verse displays the success: found me. The search displays Onesiphorus persisting until he reached Paul. The verse displays the seeking: diligent. The verse displays the Rome: searched. The verse displays the finding: achieved. Onesiphorus seeking Paul out diligently at Rome displays the depth of his devotion: when at Rome, he searched earnestly and persistently for the imprisoned apostle — braving difficulty and danger — until he found him and ministered to him. The verse displays the diligent friend, for whom Paul prays mercy in that day (v.18).
18:
"The Lord grant unto him that he may find mercy of the Lord in that day: and in how many things he ministered unto me at Ephesus, thou knowest very well." The witnesses note the prayer that Onesiphorus find mercy in that day. On "the Lord grant unto him that he may find mercy of the Lord in that day," the grammatical voices note the prayer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's prayer for his friend: "the Lord grant unto him that he may find mercy of the Lord in that day (the day of judgment)" — that Onesiphorus, who showed mercy to Paul, may himself find mercy from the Lord at the last day; mercy answering mercy.

On "in that day," the witnesses note the judgment. Barnes and Ellicott note that "that day" is the day of Christ's coming and judgment; Paul prays the highest blessing — divine mercy in the day when all is judged — upon the one who had been merciful to him.

On "in how many things he ministered unto me at Ephesus," the grammatical-historical note the prior service: "in how many things he ministered (served, helped) unto me at Ephesus, thou knowest very well" — Onesiphorus's kindness was not new; he had served Paul at Ephesus too, as Timothy well knew; a long record of faithful ministry. The verse displays the prayer that Onesiphorus find mercy in that day.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's prayer — "the Lord grant unto him that he may find mercy of the Lord in that day (of judgment)," mercy answering his mercy to Paul (Barnes, Robertson); "that day" the day of Christ's coming and judgment, the highest blessing prayed (Barnes, Ellicott); "in how many things he ministered unto me at Ephesus, thou knowest," his kindness a long record, known to Timothy (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prayer for mercy. The verse marks the prayer: find mercy. The verse displays the day: that day. The verse displays the record: ministered at Ephesus. The verse displays the witness: Timothy knows. The prayer displays Paul asking mercy for his merciful friend. The verse displays the mercy: prayed. The verse displays the day: of judgment. The verse displays the ministering: at Ephesus. The prayer that Onesiphorus find mercy in that day displays the gratitude of the apostle: he prays that the Lord would grant his faithful friend mercy at the day of judgment — for Onesiphorus had ministered to him, at Ephesus as at Rome, in many things Timothy well knew. The verse displays the apostle's grateful prayer, closing the chapter and opening the call to be strong in grace (ch.2).
